slides.title: Python Labels Everything
slides.body: Every value in Python has a **type** — a label that tells Python what kind of thing it is. The right type means Python knows what you can *do* with a value.
slides.tip: Think of types as labeled boxes: a BOOKS box and a FRAGILE box get handled very differently.

slides.title: Numbers: `int` and `float`
slides.body: Use **`int`** for whole numbers (counting) and **`float`** for decimals (measuring). Python picks the type automatically based on whether there is a decimal point.
slides.code:
age = 28        # int
price = 9.99    # float
print(age, price)
slides.tip: No decimal point → int. Has a decimal point → float.

slides.title: Text and Truth: `str` & `bool`
slides.body: A **`str`** holds any text in quotes. A **`bool`** is either `True` or `False` — Python's way of saying yes or no.
slides.tip: Bool values must be capitalized: `True` and `False`, never `true` or `false`.

slides.title: Collections: `list`, `tuple`, `dict`, `set`
slides.body: When one value is not enough, use a **collection**. `list` is editable, `tuple` is frozen, `dict` maps keys to values, and `set` holds only unique items.
slides.code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana"]  # list
person = {"name": "Ada", "age": 36}  # dict
print(fruits[0], person["name"])
slides.tip: Square brackets `[]` for lists, curly braces `{}` for dicts and sets, parentheses `()` for tuples.

slides.title: Ask Python: `type()` Tells All
slides.body: Not sure what type a value is? Call **`type()`** on it — Python answers instantly. This is your best friend when debugging.
slides.tip: Every value in Python is an object — that is why `type()` says `<class '...'>`.

slides.title: You Know Python's Core Types!
slides.body: You have met `int`, `float`, `str`, `bool`, `list`, `tuple`, `dict`, `set`, and `None`. Each is a tool for a different job — and `type()` helps you identify any of them.
slides.tip: Mutable types (list, dict, set) can change after creation; immutable ones (int, str, tuple) cannot.
